coredata.dc:title: Starch-derived carbon quantum dots: Unveiling structural insights and photocatalytic potential as a bio-sourced metal-free semiconductor
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Sheshmani, Shabnam
Mardali, Mahan
Shokrollahzadeh, Soheila
Bide, Yasamin
coredata.dc:description: The optical appeal and sustainability of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have led to these nanoparticles swiftly gaining attention and emerging as a new, multifunctional class of nanomaterials. This work centers on the hydrothermal preparation of CQDs utilizing starch, an abundant and renewable biopolymer, as the precursor. Extensive characterization via spectroscopy and microscopy techniques revealed that the starch-derived CQDs exhibit a spherical nanoscale morphology averaging a ∼ 4 nm diameter, demonstrating a red-orange photoluminescence emission. Diffuse reflectance spectroscopic analysis verified their semiconductor behavior, with an estimated direct band gap of 4.1 eV comparable to conventional semiconductors. The prepared CQDs demonstrated considerable promise as metal-free, semiconductor photocatalysts for degrading aqueous dye pollutants under UV irradiation. High photodegradation efficiencies of 45.11 %, 62.94 %, and 91.21 % were achieved for Acid Blue 21, Reactive Blue 94, and Reactive TB 133 dyes, respectively. Systematic investigations of critical process parameters like pH, CQDs dosage, dye concentration, and contact time provided vital insights into the photocatalytic mechanism. The bio-sourced CQD nanomaterials offer a sustainable pathway for effective environmental remediation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Starch
Hydrothermal
Metal-free semiconductor
Photodegradation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical structures of a) Acid Dye Blue 21, b) Reactive Blue 94, and c) Reactive Dye TB 133.
a, b) FT-IR spectra, c) Raman spectra, d) XRD patterns, e) Zeta potential, and f) mobility results of the Starch and starch-derived CQDs.
a) UV-DRS adsorption peaks, b) band gap values, and c) Photoluminescence result of CQDs.
a) TGA-DTG and b) DSC-DTA analysis of starch-derived CQDs.
a) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm, b) Langmuir plot, and c) BET analysis for as-prepared starch-derived CQDs.
HR-TEM images at different magnifications, (a) 20 nm, (b) 10 nm, (c) 5 nm, (d) SAED pattern, and e) size distribution for starch-derived CQDs.
a) EDS spectrum and b) EDS elemental mapping of carbon (in red), oxygen (in green), and their combined (red and green) for CQDs.
Effect of pH, CQDs quantity, dye concentration, and contact time on three ionic dye photodegradations.
Kinetic studies of a, c, and e) Pseudo-first-order and b, d, and f) Pseudo-second order using CQDs on Acid Dye Blue 21, Reactive Blue 94, and Reactive Dye TB 133, respectively.
Values of pore size, surface area, and pore volume in BET and BJH plots of CQDs.
EDS results for as-prepared starch-derived CQDs.

head.ce:title: Starch-derived carbon quantum dots: Unveiling structural insights and photocatalytic potential as a bio-sourced metal-free semiconductor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Basic Sciences, Yadegar-e-Imam Khomeini (RAH) Shahre Rey Branch, Islamic Azad University, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Basic Sciences, Yadegar-e-Imam Khomeini (RAH) Shahre Rey Branch, Islamic Azad University, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Technologies, Iranian Research Organization for Science and Technology (IROST), Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Iranian Research Organization for Science and Technology (IROST)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Technologies, Iranian Research Organization for Science and Technology (IROST), Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The optical appeal and sustainability of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have led to these nanoparticles swiftly gaining attention and emerging as a new, multifunctional class of nanomaterials. This work centers on the hydrothermal preparation of CQDs utilizing starch, an abundant and renewable biopolymer, as the precursor. Extensive characterization via spectroscopy and microscopy techniques revealed that the starch-derived CQDs exhibit a spherical nanoscale morphology averaging a ∼ 4 nm diameter, demonstrating a red-orange photoluminescence emission. Diffuse reflectance spectroscopic analysis verified their semiconductor behavior, with an estimated direct band gap of 4.1 eV comparable to conventional semiconductors. The prepared CQDs demonstrated considerable promise as metal-free, semiconductor photocatalysts for degrading aqueous dye pollutants under UV irradiation. High photodegradation efficiencies of 45.11 %, 62.94 %, and 91.21 % were achieved for Acid Blue 21, Reactive Blue 94, and Reactive TB 133 dyes, respectively. Systematic investigations of critical process parameters like pH, CQDs dosage, dye concentration, and contact time provided vital insights into the photocatalytic mechanism. The bio-sourced CQD nanomaterials offer a sustainable pathway for effective environmental remediation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Starch, a naturally abundant polysaccharide found in various plant sources such as corn, potatoes, and wheat, is pivotal in plant energy storage. It is a complex polymer of interconnected glucose units, giving rise to its distinctive chemical and physical properties [1]. Starch's biocompatibility, low toxicity, and environmentally friendly nature have garnered considerable attention in materials science and nanotechnology [2]. Moreover, its widespread availability and cost-effectiveness make it an attractive alternative to traditional carbon sources [3]. As a renewable and sustainable resource, starch holds excellent promise as a precursor for preparing diverse multifunctional nanomaterials. In recent years, there has been a surge of interest in preparing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from starch. Characterized by their small size (typically less than 10 nm) and fascinating optical properties, CQDs have emerged as up-and-coming nanomaterials with applications in sensing, imaging, catalysis, and optoelectronics [4,5].

Numerous methods have been explored for the preparation of CQDs. Among them, hydrothermal, microwave-assisted, and pyrolysis techniques are commonly employed. The hydrothermal method dissolves a carbon precursor in solvent and then treats it at high heat and pressure to form CQDs. This offers benefits like mild reaction settings and easy scalability for large-scale production [6]. Another promising method is microwave synthesis, which irradiates carbon sources to enable rapid, efficient heating and carbonization. This route provides shorter reaction times, better parameter control, and higher yields [7]. Pyrolysis, a thermal decomposition process, is also widely used to make CQDs. In this method, a carbon precursor is heated to high temperatures in an inert or reducing environment. Pyrolysis offers versatile reaction conditions, enabling doping or additives to tailor the CQD properties [8]. This flexibility allows for the fine-tuning of CQD characteristics for specific applications. The techniques use precise carbonization under set conditions, producing CQDs with customized sizes, surface groups, and optical properties [9–11]. By carefully tuning the parameters, researchers can obtain CQDs with desired traits for use in optoelectronics, bioimaging, sensing, and environmental fields.

Researchers have capitalized on the distinctive characteristics of CQDs in a wide range of applications [12]. These applications span optoelectronic devices, including light-emitting diodes and solar cells, as well as biosensors for detecting various analytes [13–29]. Furthermore, the photocatalytic properties of CQDs have attracted considerable attention and have paved the way for advancements in environmental remediation, water splitting, and energy conversion [30–33]. The versatility of CQDs holds great promise for addressing pressing challenges and driving innovation in multiple fields.

The treatment of wastewater contaminated with dyes is a pressing environmental issue that demands the development of sustainable and efficient photocatalysts. Traditional semiconductor-based photocatalysts face several limitations, including narrow light absorption range, high cost, and potential toxicity. Consequently, there is a growing interest in exploring metal-free alternatives derived from renewable sources that can be activated by visible light. CQDs synthesized from abundant and renewable precursors like starch have emerged as promising candidates in this regard. These CQDs offer several advantages, including broad light absorption, high photostability, and low toxicity. These characteristics render them both environmentally friendly and suitable photocatalysts for the remediation of dye-contaminated wastewater.

In this study, we synthesized starch-derived CQDs through hydrothermal carbonization and systematically investigated their performance as photocatalysts for the degradation of organic dyes under UV light irradiation. Using spectroscopy and microscopy techniques, we first characterize the obtained CQDs and then determine their effect on dye degradation, highlighting the potential of these sustainable CQDs as cost-effective and readily available photocatalysts for wastewater treatment and environmental remediation applications.

Starch (C6H10O5)n with a purity of 98 %, hydrochloric acid (HCl) with a purity of 98 %, aqueous ammonia (NH3∙H2O) with a purity of 99.9 % (25 % v/v Conc.), and ethanol (C2H5OH) with a purity of 99.8 % were obtained from Merck Co. Acid Dye Blue 21, Reactive Blue 94, and Reactive Dye TB 133 were acquired from Nordex International and D.Z.E Dye Company, UK. Acid Dye Blue 21 is a single azo dye with a molecular formula of C23H18N4Na2O9S3, a molecular weight of 636.59 g/mol, and a CAS registry number of 6441-95-8. Reactive Blue 94 belongs to the anthraquinone class of dyes, with a molecular formula of C24H12ClF2N5Na2O8S2, a molecular weight of 681.94 g/mol, and a CAS registry number of 70,416–86-3. Reactive Dye TB 133 is the copper phthalocyanine class of dyes containing four sulfonate groups. These dyes are commonly used in the textile industry, and their chemical structures are shown in Fig. 1 .

The chemical composition and properties of the CQDs were analyzed using several techniques. Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (Thermo AVATAR) identified functional groups. Raman spectroscopy (Takram P50C0R10 TEKSAN) determined vibration modes using 532 nm laser excitation. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using a PHILIPS PW1730 and indexed against the Joint Committee of Powder Diffraction Standard (JCPDS) database to elucidate the crystal structure. Zeta potential measurements (Zeta Horiba) evaluated surface charge and suspension stability. Diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) provided insights into the optical properties and electronic structure. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA-DSC Q600) assessed thermal stability. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis (BELSORP Mini II) determined surface area and pore size. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM FEI TECHNAI F20) examined morphology and particle size. Scanning electron microscopy paired with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (FESEM, TESCAN, MIRA III, made in the Czech Republic) enabled elemental analysis. Finally, UV–Vis spectroscopy (Agilent Cary 60) evaluated optical absorbance.

A hydrothermal approach was used to prepare CQDs under high-temperature, high-pressure aqueous conditions. It is known that varying key parameters, including precursor mole composition, temperature, and reaction time, influence the hydrothermal CQDs preparation process. To initiate CQDs preparation, 3 g of starch was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water and carefully transferred into a tightly sealed Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave reactor. This specialized equipment provides a controlled high-pressure environment for hydrothermal carbonization. The starch solution was heated to 200 °C and maintained at this elevated temperature for 6 h to facilitate the conversion of starch into CQDs. After completing the hydrothermal reaction, the autoclave was gradually cooled to room temperature. Then, 30 mL of ethanol was introduced into the reactor to purify the CQDs. This mixture was filtered using qualitative filter paper to remove large particles. The filtrate underwent centrifugation at 14000 rpm for 20 min to eliminate any remaining micrometer-scale carbon impurities. The supernatant was then passed through a 0.25 μm pore size syringe filter for further purification. Finally, the resulting clear, brown-colored CQDs solution was transferred to a petri dish and dried in an oven at 80 °C for one week to obtain a concentrated CQDs powder free of residual solvents (Scheme 1 ).

The photocatalytic efficiency of the as-prepared CQDs for dye degradation was evaluated through batch photodegradation experiments under UV light irradiation. Three model dyes representing different classes were tested: Acid Dye Blue 21 as an example of a single azo dye, Reactive Blue 94 as an anthraquinone-based dye, and Reactive TB 133 as a copper phthalocyanine class of dyes. The selection of Acid Dye Blue 21, Reactive Blue 94, and Reactive Dye TB 133 as pollutants in the photocatalytic degradation experiments is well-justified based on their characteristics and potential environmental impact. Acid Dye Blue 21, a widely used water-soluble azo dye in industries like textiles, leather, and paper, represents a common organic pollutant. Its vibrant blue colour and synthetic nature make it a suitable candidate for assessing the photocatalytic performance of the starch-derived carbon quantum dots, addressing a prevalent environmental concern. Reactive Blue 94 and Reactive Dye TB 133, both reactive dyes commonly employed in the textile industry, possess specific properties that make them relevant pollutants for investigation. These water-soluble dyes, belonging to the anthraquinone and copper phthalocyanine classes, respectively, are known for their colorfastness and ability to form chemical bonds with fibers. Their selection as model pollutants allows for the evaluation of the starch-derived carbon quantum dots' ability to degrade reactive dyes commonly found in textile wastewater, a significant environmental issue.

In a typical run, a 25 mL aqueous solution containing 20 mg/L of each dye was prepared at 25 °C. Then, 0.05 g of CQDs was added, and the mixture was magnetically stirred at 150 rpm for 30 min. The dye solutions were subsequently irradiated using UV light to initiate photocatalytic degradation. A 400 W mercury lamp provides broad UV–visible light and is positioned 25 cm from the sample during treatment. The dye concentration was analyzed at designated time intervals by measuring the solution absorbance using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The efficacy of starch-derived CQDs in removing the three dyes could be quantified by assessing the temporal photodegradation profiles. The results obtained from these systematic dye degradation studies provide essential information into the potential of sustainably produced, metal-free CQDs to serve as effective photocatalysts for wastewater purification and environmental remediation applications.

Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was used to characterize the functional groups and chemical structure of starch and starch-derived CQDs (Fig. 2a, b) [34,35]. One prominent peak observed in the range of approximately 3200–3600 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of hydroxyl groups (-OH) present in the starch molecule. Additionally, the region between 1400 and 1500 cm−1 exhibited distinct peaks associated with the bending vibrations of functional groups, such as CH2 and CH3. Another significant feature in the FT-IR spectrum of starch was a prominent peak appearing in the region around 1000 cm−1. This peak represents the stretching vibrations of the glycosidic linkages (-C–O–C-) that connect the individual glucose units together, forming the polymeric chain of starch. Analysis of the prepared CQDs by FT-IR spectroscopy provided valuable insights into changes in peak intensity and distribution compared to starch precursors (Fig. 2b). A significant finding was the presence of multiple peaks corresponding to the stretching vibrations of glycosidic linkages (-C–O–C-) in the FT-IR spectrum of CQDs. This observation suggests possible alterations or cleavage of glycosidic bonds during the hydrothermal preparation process of CQDs. Furthermore, new peaks emerged around 1000 cm−1, indicating various carbon‑oxygen (CO) bond vibrations. These functional groups may include hydroxyls (C–OH) and ethers (C–O–C). Furthermore, new bands from 2800 to 3000 cm−1 are attributed to carbon‑hydrogen (CH) bond stretching vibrations. Comparative FT-IR spectroscopy thus reveals valuable molecular-level insights into structural transformations that occur during hydrothermal carbonization of starch into functionalized CQDs.

Raman spectroscopy provides valuable insights into the structural and chemical properties of CQDs [35]. The Raman spectra exhibit characteristic peaks that can be categorized into two main regions, the G-band and the D-band (Fig. 2c). The G-band at around 1500–1600 cm−1 originates from the in-plane vibration of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms within the CQDs graphitic domains. The G-peak's relative intensity and spectral width indicate the degree of graphitization and the average size of the graphitic clusters, respectively. A narrower, more intense G-peak signifies enhanced graphitization and larger graphitic crystallites. Meanwhile, the D-band arising at 1200–1400 cm−1 stems from the breathing mode of A1g symmetry involving sp2 carbons mainly in disordered regions. Thus, a higher D-band intensity reflects a more significant structural disorder due to more abundant in-plane defects and distortion of the sp2 domains. The ID/IG ratio, which represents the intensity ratio between the D and G peaks, is a quantitative indicator of the level of disorder in CQDs. A higher ID/IG ratio suggests a higher degree of disorder or defects, while a lower ratio suggests a more ordered or graphitic structure. Typically, an ID/IG ratio below 1 indicates a more ordered structure with fewer defects, whereas a ratio above 1 suggests a more disordered structure. In the context of this study, the reported ID/IG ratio of 0.7 suggests that the CQDs possess a relatively ordered or graphitic structure with a lower density of defects compared to highly disordered structures. In addition, the obtained spectra displayed prominent peaks corresponding to CO stretching vibrations in the range of 1000–1200 cm−1 and CH stretching vibrations between 2800 and 3000 cm−1.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis provides insights into the crystal structure and crystalline order of materials. Starch and starch-derived CQDs exhibit distinct diffraction patterns when irradiated with X-rays (Fig. 2d) [35]. Native starches display an A-type pattern featuring a prominent double helical packing peak at around 15° (2θ), indicating well-organized granular structures enriched in amylose and amylopectin. In contrast, gelatinized or chemically modified starches show a B-type pattern lacking the 15° peak. Its broader, amorphous profile signifies disrupted double helices and reduced crystallinity from processing effects. Additionally, native and modified starches exhibit minor crystalline diffraction peaks at approx. 17, 22, 23 and 32° (2θ) angles. However, the CQD pattern consists of a single broad peak centered at ∼20° (2θ) without any sharp Bragg reflections. This confirms the absence of long-range atomic order in CQDs owing to their ultra-small carbonaceous nanodomains with dimensions of only a few nanometers. Overall, comparative XRD analysis shows quantitative information into the transition from crystalline starch precursor to amorphous CQD nanostructures during hydrothermal treatment.

Zeta potential provides information about the colloidal stability and interparticle interactions of nanoparticles in solution [36]. A high absolute Zeta potential (positive or negative) indicates stronger electrostatic repulsion between particles, enhancing dispersion stability by preventing aggregation. In contrast, a low absolute value signifies weaker repulsive forces and a greater tendency for particle aggregation due to van der Waals attraction. The hydrothermally prepared CQDs in this study exhibited a negative Zeta-potential of −20 to −80 mV (Fig. 2e). This net negative surface charge arises from deprotonation of oxygen-containing functional groups like carboxyls and hydroxyls abundantly present on the CQDs surface. At neutral pH, these groups ionized to yield negatively charged moieties such as carboxylates, enhancing the electronegativity of the CQDs-solution interface. The resultant net negative charge creates a repulsive force between individual CQDs, overcoming van der Waals attraction and promoting colloidal stability. Also, the negative mobility signifies an overall negative surface charge on CQDs that causes them to move towards the positive electrode under an applied electric field during zeta potential analysis (Fig. 2f).

Diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) is a valuable technique for probing the optical absorption and reflection properties of materials [36]. DRS results provided insight into the effective band gap of the CQDs (Fig. 3a, b). The band gap represents the minimum energy threshold for photoexcitation of electrons from the valence band (highest occupied molecular orbitals, HOMOs) to the conduction band (lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals, LUMOs). The DRS spectrum depicts the variation in the intensity of diffused reflectance as a function of incident photon wavelength. Tauc plot analysis is widely utilized to estimate the optical band gap energy (Eg) from the DRS data. In this method, the absorption coefficient (α) is plotted against the photon energy (eV) and then linearly extrapolated to α = 0. The intercept on the photon energy axis yields Eg according to the nature of the optical transition. A direct allowed transition is assumed for CQDs, and (αhν)2 vs. hν is plotted based on Tauc's formula. The estimated Eg via Tauc plot for the hydrothermally prepared CQDs is approximately 4.1 eV. This wide band gap confirms the semiconductor behavior of CQDs.

CQDs exhibit intriguing photoluminescence (PL) properties, emitting visible light upon photoexcitation [37]. This fluorescence arises from quantum confinement effects in their unique electronic band structure. Incident photons with energy equal to or greater than the CQDs band gap get absorbed, exciting electrons from the valence band to conduction band states. Subsequent relaxation and radiative recombination of these electron-hole pairs leads to light emission at wavelengths dictated by the band gap. The prepared CQDs demonstrated excitation-dependent PL under UV light (Fig. 3c). When excited at 300 nm, a PL peak centered at 600 nm (orange-red) is observed. This red-shifted emission indicates increasing CQD sizes with smaller band gaps than blue-emitting smaller dots. During preparation, the PL colour can be tuned by controlling properties like size, composition, and surface chemistry.

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and derivative thermogravimetry (DTG) reveal the thermal stability and decomposition behavior of materials [38]. As illustrated in Fig. 4a, the TGA of the starch-derived CQDs shows two distinct weight loss events. The first weight loss at 202 °C can be ascribed to removing volatile species adsorbed on the CQDs surfaces and cleavage of weakly bound surface functional groups. The more pronounced weight reduction at 400 °C signifies the thermal decomposition of the CQDs core structures. This relatively high decomposition temperature highlights the excellent thermal stability of the starch-derived CQDs. By determining the characteristic temperatures corresponding to changes in sample weight, the TGA and DTG provided information crucial for assessing the suitability of newly prepared CQDs for high-temperature processes and applications. The thermal stability demonstrated by these starch-CQDs makes them promising candidates for robust, carbon-based nanomaterials. Fig. 4b displays the differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) and differential thermal analysis (DTA) thermograms obtained for the starch-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs). DSC measures the heat flow associated with phase transitions in a sample, while DTA detects temperature differences between the sample and reference as a function of temperature. A distinct endothermic peak centered at 400 °C can be observed in the overlapped DSC-DTA profiles. This endotherm signifies the absorption of 5414 J/g of heat by processes occurring within the CQDs. Plausible events contributing to this endotherm include melting, evaporation, or degradation reactions. By pinpointing temperatures at which heat is absorbed or released, DSC and DTA characterization shed light on phase changes and thermal transitions taking place in CQDs. The high temperature of the endotherm suggests excellent thermal stability of the starch-derived CQDs. The quantitative enthalpy measurement also provides critical insights into the energy-dependent behavior of CQDs for applications requiring heat transfer.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) revealed spherical CQDs with an average diameter of approximately 4 nm, homogeneously distributed across the sample (Fig. 6 ). The narrow size distribution indicates controlled nucleation and growth during preparation, producing uniform CQDs. High-resolution TEM imaging further exposed the crystalline structure of individual CQDs, with visible lattice fringes corresponding to atomic planes within the nanocrystals. These ordered arrangements lead to quantum confinement effects that tune the optical and electronic properties of CQDs. The ability to tailor size during preparation provides a means to engineer desired quantum effects. With well-defined dimensions verified by TEM and evidence of crystallinity, this technique confirms both morphological and structural characteristics that are foundational to emerging applications of CQDs across diverse fields, such as bioimaging, photocatalysis, and optoelectronics.

EDS allows elemental analysis of CQDs by detecting the characteristic X-rays emitted from elements present upon irradiation with an electron beam. EDS analysis generated an emission spectrum and elemental map of the CQDs, revealing the dominant presence of carbon (48.04 % by weight) and oxygen (51.96 % by weight), as shown in Fig. 7 and Table 2 .

Photodegradation studies have been carried out in recent years [39–44]. The photodegradation efficiencies of three anionic dyes, Acid Dye Blue 21, Reactive Blue 94, and Reactive Dye TB 133, were evaluated over a pH range from 2 to 6 using CQDs (Fig. 8 ). The CQDs exhibited superior dye degradation at a more acidic pH, with removal percentages of 45.11, 35.87, and 69.26 % at pH 2 for Acid Dye Blue 21, Reactive Blue 94, and Reactive Dye TB 133, respectively. Increasing the pH to more neutral values considerably decreased the degradation efficiency to 5–17 % at pH 6, depending on the dye. This pH-dependent photodegradation suggests that electrostatic interactions govern dye degradation [45,46]. In acidic media, the CQDs surfaces carry a positive charge from protonated oxygen functionalities, while the dyes are negatively charged. This cation-anion interaction promotes dye removal onto the photoactive CQD surfaces through favorable electrostatics. Under acidic conditions, an abundance of H+ ions leads to an increase in the surface charge and the possibility of complexation [47–49]. As the pH increases, deprotonation of the CQD surface reduces the positive charge density, weakening this attractive force. Consequently, higher pH systems demonstrate inferior photodegradation. Tuning the electrostatic environment to favor degradation proves critical for optimizing the interfacial contact between the CQDs and dye pollutants, enabling efficient photooxidative degradation.

The influence of CQDs dosage on the photodegradation of the three anionic dyes is depicted in Fig. 8. Increasing the CQDs mass from 0.01 to 0.03 g enhanced the removal efficiencies from 34 to 39.92 %, 22.53 to 32.23 %, and 76.67 to 77.68 % for Acid Dye Blue 21, Reactive Blue 94, and Reactive Dye TB 133, respectively. This augmentation of degradation performance correlates with the greater availability of surface reactive sites and overall photon absorption offered by the heightened CQDs concentration. However, a further dosage increase to 0.05 g induced a decline in efficiency for all dyes. This inverted trend suggests that excessive CQDs may shield light penetration or induce aggregation, reducing photoactivated surface area. An optimal mass exists where sufficient photons and reactive sites can degrade the maximum amount of dye without limitation due to the effects of overcrowding. For the prepared CQDs, this optimum dosage was 0.03 g. Identifying the ideal CQDs loading to harness photogenerated carriers and reactive species for superior photooxidation without introducing mass transport restrictions provides pivotal guidance for implementing these visible-light photocatalysts in purification applications [50].

The influence of initial dye concentration on the photodegradation performance is presented in Fig. 8. For all three anionic dyes, the removal efficiency followed an inverse correlation with dye concentration. Acid Dye Blue 21, Reactive Blue 94, and Reactive Dye TB 133 exhibited optimal degradation of 42.51, 22.36, and 91.21 %, respectively, at the lowest 5 ppm feed concentration. Increasing the dye content to 10 and 30 ppm induced a noticeable decay in photodegradation. At the highest 30 ppm dosage, the efficiency declined to 38.66, 8.85, and 64.28 % for each dye. The superior degradation at lower concentrations can be attributed to greater light penetration, which activates the CQD catalysts and reactive species generation. In contrast, excessive dye quantities can impede light absorption while overwhelming the available active sites with excess pollutant molecules competing for photooxidation. Therefore, an optimized dye input concentration is imperative to balance an adequate interface between dye molecules and photocatalyst surface area with sufficient photon flux for catalysis without becoming limiting. Identifying this ideal concentration enables optimized utilization of the powerful photooxidation capacity of CQDs [46].

The influence of irradiation time on the photodegradation of the dyes over the CQDs was investigated at 30, 60, and 90 min, as presented in Fig. 8. For Acid Dye Blue 21, extending the contact period had negligible impact, with near-constant removal levels around 38 %. This plateau suggests that the photodegradation reaches saturation within 30 min. In contrast, Reactive Blue 94 exhibited progressively higher degradation with more prolonged exposure, escalating from 36.32 % to 62.94 % at 30 and 90 min, respectively. The steady improvement implies the continuous generation of reactive species by the photoexcited CQDs over extended illumination. However, an inverse trend was displayed by Reactive Dye TB 133, with optimal elimination of 74.27 % at 30 min, diminishing to 61.61 % after 90 min. The reduced performance indicates that dye degradation products may passivate the CQD surface or attenuate light over time. Elucidating the timescale dependence and limiting mechanisms is imperative for implementing CQD photocatalysts efficiently. The variance in optimal contact time among the dyes highlights the importance of customized illumination periods for contaminated systems [46].

Kinetic models, such as the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order equations, are commonly utilized to assess the efficiency of dye photodegradation. The first-order and second-order kinetic models are shown in Eqs. (1) and (2). In these equations, qe and qt represent the adsorption capacity at equilibrium and at different times, respectively.
(1)

q e

q t
=
lnq e
−
k 1
t
(2)
t /
q t
= 1 / (
k 2
q eq
2
t /
q Eq

To clarify the kinetic behavior governing the CQDs-mediated photodegradation of the anionic dyes, the process was analyzed using pseudo-first order and pseudo-second order models (Fig. 9 ). The pseudo-first order equation assumes a rate dependence solely on dye concentration in contrast to the pseudo-second order which considers the combined effect of both dye and CQDs concentrations. A comparison of the linear regression fit indicates that the pseudo-second order kinetics provides a superior correlation for Acid Dye Blue 21 and Reactive Dye TB 133, with excellent R2 values of 0.99. This suggests that the photodegradation rate of these dyes involves both adsorbed dye molecules and interaction with CQD catalytic sites. In contrast, Reactive Blue 94 follows pseudo-first order kinetics more closely (R2 = 0.88), implying surface-independent photolysis may dominate. Discerning the kinetic models offers vital mechanistic insights into interfacial redox processes governing the photocatalytic activity. The prevalence of pseudo-second order behavior highlights the critical role of synergistic effects between the excited CQDs and adsorbed substrates in facilitating the unique artificial enzyme-mimetic catalytic functions.

This study successfully demonstrated the preparation of CQDs from starch via a facile hydrothermal route. Extensive physicochemical characterization revealed critical properties of the CQDs, including a crystalline structure, spherical morphology with an average size of around 4 nm, robust thermal stability up to 400 °C, and an optical band gap of 4.1 eV. Notably, the CQDs exhibited promising photocatalytic activity for the degradation of anionic dyes under UV irradiation. Systematic investigation of critical process parameters offered valuable mechanistic insights into dye photodegradation. Favorable degradation efficiencies up to 91.21 % were achieved under optimized conditions of acidic pH, moderate photocatalyst loading around 0.03 g/L, dye concentrations below 10 ppm, and illumination times from 30 to 90 min, depending on the dye system. A comparison of pseudo-first order and pseudo-second order kinetic models suggested that adsorption-coupled interfacial redox reactions dominate the photocatalytic mechanism. Overall, this work successfully prepared sustainable starch-derived CQDs and demonstrated their potential as metal-free semiconductor photocatalysts for wastewater treatment applications. The findings open exciting possibilities for leveraging these earth-abundant bio-sourced nanomaterials for environmental remediation. With continued development, CQDs can offer a low-cost, eco-friendly solution to pressing water purification challenges.

Shabnam Sheshmani: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Mahan Mardali: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Soheila Shokrollahzadeh: Methodology, Investigation. Yasamin Bide: Methodology, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is based upon research funded by Iran National Science Foundation (INSF) under project No. 4013521.
